exemplar.title: To what extent does the daily concentration of PM2.5 particulate matter  (measured at 8:00a.m and 15:30 p.m) correlate with the severity of reported respiratory symptoms among DP students at SNA school in Binh Chanh District, Ho Chi Minh City, over a 4-week period

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the daily concentration of PM2.5 particulate matter  (measured at 8:00a.m and 15:30 p.m) correlate with the severity of reported respiratory symptoms among DP students at SNA school in Binh Chanh District, Ho Chi Minh City, over a 4-week period

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report follows IB IA structure with clear sections and subheadings
Appropriate ESS and statistical terminology are used correctly
Overall argument flows logically from context through to conclusions
weaknesses: Inconsistent structure with cluttered tables and formatting issues interrupt flow
Typographical artifacts and OCR symbols reduce concision and professionalism
Citation formatting is uneven across references
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and organization, terminology/concision, and logic/coherence each align with the band-2 descriptors: present but inconsistently sustained, with formatting and clarity issues. Therefore a holistic communication score of 2 is justified.

strengths: Research question is precisely stated, specifying variables, location, population and timeframe
Clear overview of global and local PMâ.â context with reputable sources and key term definitions
Comprehensive explanation of connections between traffic-derived PMâ.â and respiratory impacts
weaknesses: Minor inconsistency regarding two- vs four-week duration of the study
Environmental issue labeled too generically as âAir pollutionâ rather than urban PMâ.â sources
Meteorological influences on PMâ.â dispersion are not fully explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands met the 5â6 band descriptors: a focused, testable research question; a detailed, contextualised environmental issue; and comprehensive connections between issue and question. Minor formatting and scope inconsistencies prevent a topâband (6), but the work clearly demonstrates strong contextualisation and coherence, justifying a score of 5.

strengths: Method outline is repeatable with fixed times, locations and survey instrument
Risk and ethical considerations are thoroughly addressed, including consent, anonymity and participant rights
Strong justification for controlling weather conditions to reduce confounding variables
weaknesses: Method relies on a smartphone app rather than a calibrated PMâ.â monitor
Convenience sampling of five students lacks statistical representativeness
Sampling strategy lacks GPS detail for reproducibility
Variables table omits management justification for some factors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design and sampling fit the middle (3â4) band: repeatable but limited in data sufficiency and representativeness. Ethical/risk considerations meet the top band. Balancing these, the overall planning quality is solid but constrained, meriting a holistic score of 4.

strengths: Discussion links findings to local/global airâquality challenges and SDGs
Strengths and weaknesses of the method are identified in a structured table
Improvement suggestions address sample size, duration and monitoring methods
weaknesses: Limited critical evaluation of how conclusions fulfil the research question and null hypothesis
Measurement-accuracy issues (app vs. calibrated monitor) are not discussed
Improvement proposals lack detailed, feasible implementation plans
No prioritisation of which weaknesses most impact validity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall into the 3â4 band: evaluations are meaningful but lack depth and critical prioritisation, and improvements are relevant but not fully developed. The overall discussion and evaluation quality warrants a score of 3.

strengths: Constructed appropriate graphs and tables with axes, units and trendlines
Correct calculation of means, exceedance percentages and Pearson r values
Clear percentage exceedance table and correct daily average formula
Transparent inclusion of Excel correlation calculations and summary table
weaknesses: Some tables are incomplete or misformatted, and axis labels need enlargement
Misinterpretation of r-value (e.g., r=0.046 described as negative)
No significance testing and confusion between r and RÂ²
Bulky, unlabelled correlation tables and missing computed cells
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation achieved the 3â4 band, but data analysis and conclusion drawing were only âcorrect but incompleteâ (band 3). Omissions in statistical testing and interpretation errors constrain the overall quality, leading to a holistic score of 3.

strengths: Application proposals link short-term and long-term solutions to findings and legislation with clear justification
weaknesses: Evaluation of solution strengths and weaknesses is selective, lacking comprehensive cost, feasibility or stakeholder analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The proposal strand meets full marks (3), but solution evaluation is partial (band 2). Combined performance sits below the top band, yielding an overall applications score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key ESS and statistical terms (e.g., PM2.5, Pearson correlation) are used correctly, but numerous typographical symbols, superfluous decoration and occasional grammatical lapses reduce concision.  Thus the work meets either terminology or concision, not consistently both â band 2.

The discussion links findings to local and global air-quality challenges and SDGs, recognising that correlations are modest.  However, the evaluation of how well the conclusion answers the research question and the null hypothesis is limited and some claims remain general.  Fits descriptor 3-4 (score 3).

The student states that the hypothesis is supported by moderate/weak positive correlations and references specific r values and graphs; this interprets trends with partial validity.  Limitations and alternative explanations are only briefly noted, so the conclusion is not fully supported.  Hence middle band, score 3.

Mean values, WHO exceedance percentages and Pearson r values are calculated correctly; however, significance is not tested and some confusion between r and RÂ² is evident.  Confounding variables are not incorporated into the analysis.  Thus analysis is âcorrect but incompleteâ, meriting the lower half of the 3-4 band (3).

Suggested improvements (longer study, larger sample, continuous monitoring, objective health measures) are relevant and would address several weaknesses, but they are not developed into detailed, feasible plans.  Thus they are âmeaningfulâ rather than âsignificantâ, earning a 3.

A repeatable outline is provided (fixed times, device, location, survey instrument), but reliance on a phone app rather than a calibrated onsite monitor and the very small human sample mean the method is unlikely to gather âsufficientâ data for strong conclusions.  Hence it satisfies the 3-4 descriptor (adequate but limited).

Links between traffic-derived PM2.5, WHO exceedance figures, the schoolâs timetable, and potential student exposure are explicitly explained.  The student clarifies why morning and afternoon peak measurements coincide with respiratory impacts, demonstrating a comprehensive explanation of connections.  Minor gaps (e.g., meteorological influence not fully traced) keep the mark at 5 rather than 6.

The research question is explicitly stated, includes the variables (PM2.5 levels at two stated times and respiratory-symptom severity), identifies the place, population and time scale, and is framed as a testable correlation study.  The wording is sufficiently narrow to allow realistic data collection and analysis while remaining environmentally relevant.  Minor internal inconsistencies about a two- vs four-week duration do not undermine overall focus, so the strand fits the 5-6 band, lower end.

Some potential strengths (legal alignment, health benefits) and challenges (need for stricter vehicle standards) are mentioned, but the analysis is selective and does not examine cost, feasibility or stakeholder perspectives comprehensively.  Partial evaluation â 2 marks.

The report follows the IB IA structure with identifiable sections and sub-headings, yet raw-data tables are cluttered and some figures interrupt flow.  Organization is present but not sustained perfectly, matching band 2.

Strengths and limitations are listed in a table, mentioning sample size, confounding variables and ecological validity.  Discussion lacks depth (e.g., no comment on measurement accuracy of the app) and does not prioritise which weaknesses have greatest impact, so descriptor 3-4 applies (score 3).

The student gives a clear overview of global PM2.5 pollution, cites WHO limits, presents local AQI data, and explains the proximity of a busy avenue to the school.  These details place the study firmly within a well-defined environmental issue relevant to human health and urban air quality.  The discussion moves beyond a brief outline to a contextualised narrative; therefore the descriptor for 5-6 is met (score 5).

Sampling is described as convenience sampling of five DP students with age range and justification for manageability.  No statistical rationale or representativeness argument is offered.  This is a description rather than a justification, fitting the mid-band (3).

The student details informed-consent procedures, anonymity, right to withdraw, and low-risk nature of the survey.  Practical burdens on participants are minimised and explained.  Risks from outdoor measurement are negligible but still acknowledged.  Coverage is thorough, meeting the 5-6 descriptor (score 5).

Tables of summary statistics and five scatter plots are provided; axes, units and trendlines are present.  Some tables are incomplete or contain formatting errors, but the essential data are shown and the reader can follow patterns.  Therefore charts are âappropriate with some omissionsâ â top of the 3-4 band.

The proposal combines short-term (schedule changes, purifiers) and long-term (green spaces, emission standards) actions, linked to findings and to national legislation, which constitutes a clear justification.  Therefore full marks for this strand (3).

The argument generally progresses logically from context to method, results and discussion, but inconsistencies (two- vs four-week study, mislabelled tables) and formatting issues impede smooth reading in places.  This corresponds to âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ (band 2).

annotations.comment: Labeling the environmental issue as âAir pollutionsâ is too generic. Specify urban PMâ.â sources and local relevance for clarity.
Introduction contextualises global PMâ.â impacts effectively, citing reputable sources and defining key terms, enhancing reader understanding of the issue.
WHO guideline paragraph cites thresholds well, but consider breaking long sentences for readability and ensure consistent Âµg/mÂ³ formatting.
The research question is precisely stated, specifying variables, location, population and timeframe, which sets a focused, coherent study scope.
Null hypothesis is explicitly stated, providing a clear basis for statistical testing and ensuring methodological rigor.
The variables table omits management justification for the âStudent factorsâ row; complete this entry to strengthen variable control.
Local connection to traffic patterns and school schedule is coherently explained, demonstrating strong link between context and research question.
The hypothesis is clear and testable, articulating expected positive correlation between PMâ.â levels and symptom severity.
Measurement locations description is clear, but specify exact GPS points or mapping for reproducibility and spatial consistency.
Sampling strategy description states convenience sampling of five DP students but lacks justification of representativeness; justify choice to meet higher band.
Risk and ethical considerations are comprehensively addressed with consent, withdrawal rights, anonymity and minimal burden.
Graphs 3â5 persist logical progression in analysis, showing each symptom correlation across consistent 4-week data.
Analysis text notes r-values well but mistakenly describes r=0.046 as negative; correct interpretation to maintain analytical accuracy.
Justification for controlling weather conditions is strong, acknowledging its impact on PMâ.â dispersion and confounding variables.
Materials list relies solely on a smartphone app rather than a calibrated PMâ.â monitor; consider accessing a reference-grade instrument for greater accuracy.
The Google Form setup includes unnecessary symbols (e.g., ââ ââ¼â). Remove OCR artifacts and ensure text is concise.
Summary statistics table has empty cells and lacks clear exceedance thresholds; complete all fields and label units consistently.
Percentage exceedance table clearly presents morning/afternoon exceedances relative to WHO guidelines, aiding interpretation.
The formula for PMâ.â daily average is correctly shown, demonstrating appropriate use of mathematical notation.
Correlation calculation table is bulky and unlabelled rows make following the Excel CORREL function difficult; streamline and annotate key results.
Including a screenshot of the Excel CORREL calculation strengthens transparency of data analysis procedures.
Correlation results table is prepared but lacks the computed r-values; populate all cells and provide concise interpretation.
Correlation summary table on page 10 presents r-values and interpretations clearly, facilitating quick pattern recognition.
Scatter plots effectively illustrate PMâ.â vs symptom severity, but axis labels and legends could be enlarged for readability.
Local/global context conclusion links findings to SDGs effectively but could evaluate hypothesis fulfilment more critically.
Evaluation/improvement table is concise but improvement suggestions lack detail; expand with specific, feasible modifications.
Application section proposes schedule adjustments and purifiers well, but add cost/feasibility discussion to strengthen justification.
Citation entries need consistent formatting (journal names, italics), ensure uniform style across all references.
OCR artifacts (unrecognized symbols) appear in form sections; clean text and verify survey content for clarity and professionalism.
